ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific lemon tea subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000A covers tea and tea-based beverages as a category; no specific lemon tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 represents tea as a substance; lemon tea is not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of cold lemon tea is approximately 300 ml (300 g), equivalent to a typical takeaway cup served at Singaporean and Hong Kong-style beverage stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon Tea, ready-to-drink, sweetened — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for sweetened tea beverages
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Singaporean cold lemon tea exists in a single approved source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for sweetened lemon tea (NDB 14355 / FDC ID 171890, 'Tea, ready-to-drink, lemon, sweetened') and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for sweetened tea beverages. The restaurant version (Hai Kah Lang) is a freshly brewed variant; sugar content may be higher than commercial RTD products. Values represent a moderate-sugar freshly brewed lemon tea at approximately 6–8 g sugar per 100 ml, consistent with typical Singaporean hawker-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened tea beverages — analogous estimate based on 'Tea, black, sweetened with sugar' GI data; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cold lemon tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: sweetened black tea with lemon, which has a GI in the range of 35–42 due to the moderate sucrose content and the presence of tea polyphenols that may attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 19.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 7.4; rounded to 11.7 using 6.5 g/100g × 300g = 19.5 g carbohydrate per serving: GL = 38 × 19.5 / 100 = 7.4. Note: GL corrected to 7.4 — see notes field.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 100 ml); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Cold lemon tea is low in sodium (~15 mg per 300 ml serving) and poses minimal direct risk for hypertension; however, individuals should monitor total sugar intake as high-sugar diets may contribute to elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content (~6.3 g per 100 ml; ~19 g per 300 ml serving) raises postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid sweetened cold lemon tea due to its added sugar content (~19 g per 300 ml serving), which can elevate postprandial blood glucose; requesting a reduced-sugar or unsweetened version is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat; moderate sugar content is the primary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cold lemon tea contains no fat or saturated fat and is low in sodium, making it a low cardiovascular risk beverage in terms of lipid profile; however, regular consumption of the sweetened version may contribute to excess caloric intake and should be moderated in a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cold Lemon Tea (柠檬茶, 冷) is a popular chilled tea-based beverage widely consumed in Singapore, Hong Kong, and across Chinese-influenced Southeast Asian food culture. It is prepared by brewing black tea (commonly Ceylon or Chinese black tea), allowing it to cool, and then combining it with freshly squeezed or concentrated lemon juice and sugar or simple syrup, served over ice. The result is a refreshing, lightly tart, and moderately sweet drink with a golden-amber colour and a clean citrus aroma. The texture is thin and watery, similar to iced tea, with a pleasant balance of astringency from the tea tannins and brightness from the lemon acid. In Singapore, cold lemon tea is a staple at Chinese zi char restaurants, hawker centres, and modern beverage stalls. It is commonly ordered alongside hot pot meals, as the acidity and coolness provide a palate-cleansing contrast to rich, spicy broths. Nutritionally, cold lemon tea is low in calories (~26 kcal per 100 ml), contains negligible protein and fat, and derives most of its energy from added sugar (~6.3 g per 100 ml). It provides a small amount of vitamin C from lemon juice (~3 mg per 100 ml) and trace minerals including potassium (~42 mg per 100 ml). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 38 (low GI), though the glycemic load per 300 ml serving is moderate (~7.4 g) due to the sugar content. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced-sugar versions, as a standard 300 ml serving may contain approximately 19 g of sugar. The beverage is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal or gluten-free diets. It contains no dairy, no allergens of concern, and is naturally cholesterol-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lemon tea?
Is lemon tea good for diabetics?
How much sugar is in cold lemon tea?
Can I drink lemon tea if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of lemon tea?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Singaporean freshly brewed cold lemon tea exists in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for sweetened ready-to-drink lemon tea and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for sweetened tea beverages. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened black tea. Confidence is medium rather than high because the restaurant-specific preparation (Hai Kah Lang, Funan Mall) may differ in sugar concentration from the reference values used.
notes: 1. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero, as lemon tea is a water-based beverage containing no fat-bearing ingredients. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171890). 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are 0.0: naturally zero by product composition. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as the beverage contains no intact plant fibre; lemon juice contributes negligible fibre in liquid form. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero; lemon tea contains no vitamin D. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1.0: not reported in the USDA FoodData Central reference entry for sweetened lemon tea. 8. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 9. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 10. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 11. vitamin_b6_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 12. folate_mcg_dfe is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 13. vitamin_e_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 14. phosphorus_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 15. magnesium_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 16. zinc_mg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 17. selenium_mcg is -1.0: not reported in the reference source. 18. glycemic_index set to 38 (low GI) as an analogous estimate for sweetened black tea with lemon; gi_category low per University of Sydney GI Database analogy. glycemic_load_per_serving corrected to 7.4 (GL = 38 × 19.5 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving / 100 = 7.4); the gi_source notes field contained a transcription error showing 11.7 which is incorrect — the correct GL is 7.4. 19. protein_g is 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting trace protein from tea solids and lemon juice; this is not zero. 20. The restaurant source (Hai Kah Lang, Funan Mall, GrabFood Singapore) is a seed record only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source per Project Veritas source policy. 21. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact lemon tea codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT.
